Forum Classicum 59 (2016): 128-43. Davis "revisits the issue of the nature and scope of Aeneas' angry outburst from both an ethnographic and philosophical perspective. The execution of Turnus is shown to be totally consistent with, even required by, Roman norms of pietas and its core obligations. In terms of the ethical implications of the episode, the violent ira displayed by Aeneas is fundamentally consonant with Epicurean thought on the subject of justified anger-a subject with which Vergil was demonstrably steeped" (169). Geymonat writes about the unification of the Trojans with the Latins in Aen. 12. Loar proposes "that Vergil uses the image of a triumphant Hercules [at Aen. 8.200-204] as a way to tap into a rich layer of Republican triumphal history that is instantiated in Rome's monuments, portraying Augustus ultimately as one more in the long line of notable Republican triumphatores" (46). Davis "revisits the issue of the nature and scope of Aeneas' angry outburst
from both an ethnographic and philosophical perspective. The execution
of Turnus is shown to be totally consistent with, even required by, Roman
norms of pietas and its core obligations. In terms of the ethical implications of
the episode, the violent ira displayed by Aeneas is fundamentally consonant
with Epicurean thought on the subject of justified anger-a subject with
which Vergil was demonstrably steeped" (169). Geymonat writes about the
unification of the Trojans with the Latins in Aen. 12. Loar proposes "that
Vergil uses the image of a triumphant Hercules [at Aen. 8.200-204] as a way
to tap into a rich layer of Republican triumphal history that is instantiated
in Rome's monuments, portraying Augustus ultimately as one more in the
long line of notable Republican triumphatores" (46). Suerbaum considers
the experiences of the Trojans in the second half of the Aeneid from the
perspective of contemporary refugees.

Cugusi discusses the heterogeneous character of twenty-eight inscriptional poems from Roman Britain dating from the first through sixth centuries, some of which contain Vergilian echoes. Guittard contemplates the concept of the Augustan return of the Golden Age in Vergil's Eclogues, Georgics, and Aeneid. Nelis studies the ways in which the names Maecenas, Vergilius, and Caesar function within the Georgics and how they illuminate the relationship between the poem and its historical context. Pagliara untangles the complex literary tradition concerning the Ausones of ancient Italy: the ethnic Ausones are mentioned in historical and geographical sources on Campania; but in Greek poetry Ausonia occurs simply as a synonym for Italy. Vergil first uses Ausonii of the peoples allied with Turnus, then extends the term to all the people included in Aeneas's pax, "thus foreshadowing the future pax Augusta; finally, with a natural shift, he applies it to the Romans, who inherit all that world." Cugusi discusses the heterogeneous character of twenty-eight inscriptional poems from Roman Britain dating from the first through sixth centuries, some of which contain Vergilian echoes. Goldschmidt's paper focuses on the ways in which Horace, Vergil, and Livy responded to Carthage and the Punic Wars. Pagliara untangles the complex literary tradition concerning the Ausones of ancient Italy: the ethnic Ausones are mentioned in historical and geographical sources on Campania; but in Greek poetry Ausonia occurs simply as a synonym for Italy. Vergil first uses Ausonii of the peoples allied with Turnus, then extends the term to all the people included in Aeneas's pax, "thus foreshadowing the future pax Augusta; finally, with a natural shift, he applies it to the Romans, who inherit all that world." Thus "Virgil inserts in his erudite reconstruction of the ancient peopling Vergilian Bibliography - 167 of Italy the old Greek notion of Ausones, which he borrowed in its usefully adaptable semantic vagueness from his Hellenistic poetic sources." Foster argues that Servius's commentaries on Vergil provide insight into
what might have happened in his classroom. His teaching had to enable
students who did not all speak Latin as a native language to recognize
both archaic and contemporary Latin and to distinguish between different
formal registers, including prose and verse. Lunelli discusses a Vergilian
scholion in the margin of Verona, Biblioteca Capitolare, MS XL (olim 38),
fol. 253r, with its controversial claim for the existence of a cult of Apollo
near Padua. Peirano considers how ancient authorial ascriptions function
as paratexts, claiming authority for the text even when that authority may
sometimes be faked. Smith observes that Cato is cited by Servius more than
any other historian, "and looked at the other way round, Servius is a very
important source for our knowledge of Cato." His study asks "what we learn
Vergilian Bibliography - 175
from Servius' use of Cato, and what we learn about Virgil." Squillante writes
that Vergil's favorable depiction of the cities of Italy was a way of honoring
the Augustan program. This perspective influenced later Latin literature
from Pliny to Claudian to Namatianus and is amplified in Servius and other
late antique commentaries in the context of the revival of imperial ideology
in the late fourth and early fifth centuries. Stok's goal is to reconstruct
the Vergilian text commented on by Servius Danielis (DS). He considers
instances where DS proposes a lemma different from that of Servius;
lemmas in DS not commented on by Servius; and cases in which DS reports
variants of Vergil's transmitted text.

Bernabò, in a reassessment that is "entirely an art historian investigation,"
discusses the Vergilius Romanus (Rome, Biblioteca Apostolica Vaticana,
cod. Vat. Lat. 3867. "Unconvinced of the claims that the manuscript was
produced either in Italy (i.e., Rome or Ravenna) or Constantinople, I argue
for an origin in a Western province of the Roman Empire, probably southern
Gaul" (239). Dickey discusses "bilingual papyrus texts of Vergil and Cicero,
with the Latin original and a Greek translation arranged in distinctive
narrow columns," which provide "a unique window" into the experience
of nonnative Latin speakers learning to read the classics of Latin literature.
This system of exegesis "was fundamental to ancient learning" but has "been
overlooked and misunderstood in modern times, in part because it has no
parallel in modern teaching or scholarship" (807). Columnar translation
probably developed from columnar glossaries. Fusi endorses Heinsius's
emendation of "variisque" to "Phariisque" at Aen. 8.685. Huyck clarifies
Turnus's furious speech to his men in Aen. 9, who are shaken after having
witnessed the transformation of the Trojan ships, by emendation. "The
difficulty of construing the words penitus … / femineum (141-142), whether

with non or the variant nunc, has long been noted by commentators." He
proposes reading "'sed periisse semel satis est': peccare fuisset / ante satis.
<pereat>, penitus modo, nunc genus omne / femineum!" This translates
to "'But it's enough (for them) to have perished once,' (someone will say).
(Then) it should have been enough (for them) to persue their adulteries
beforehand. May the whole womanish race perish here and now, provided
(that it perish) for good!" He adduces Il. 9.375-77 as "a previously undetected
model" (245). Vallat regards the Latin and Greek explanatory glosses found

Ciampolo di Meo degli Ugurgieri, the subject of Lagomarsini's study, was
the first to translate the Aeneid into Italian. O'Sullivan finds in the glosses
of the ninth-century Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS Auct. F.2.8 "a strong
endorsement of pagan learning" and an interest in encyclopedism. The
glosses show "that it was not only Vergil's style but also his content that
engaged Christian readers." This conclusion "challenges the older view of
confrontation between pagan and Christian cultures."

Guizzi notes that the idea that Crete was deserted after the Trojan War
(Aen. 3.121-23), not mentioned by Homer, was later picked up on by Dante,
Inferno 14.99. Hardie "examines the classical, above all Virgilian, models for
Adamastor, the monstrous personification of the Cape of Good Hope who
utters a curse against Vasco da Gama's sailor" (281) in Luís Vaz de Camões's
Lusiads (os Lusíadas), a Portuguese epic poem published in 1572 on Vasco
da Gama's voyage to India.

Alonso Fernández reads the libretto of La Didone, a coreodramma (dramatic
ballet) by the Italian choreographer and composer Salvatore Viganò (1769-
1821) that reconfigured the Dido story in Aen. 1 and 4, with attention to
the corporeal and kinaesthetic potential in Vergil's story. She argues that
"these traces of bodily movement and expressivity were motivated by the …
performance culture in Augustan times, in particular, by the growing trend
of pantomime dancing" (400). Eastin interprets the visual anachronims in
the first illustrated print edition of Vergil's works, "published in Strasbourg
in 1502 under the direction of the German humanist Sebastian Brant"
(1457-1521). The anachronisms "serve as visual translations that capture
the greater message of Virgil's poetry and so help to interpret the social,
political, and artistic concerns of both Virgil and Brant" (85). Gesztelyi
demonstrates that the Julio-Claudian Sebasteion built in Aphrodisias
contains reliefs depicting scenes from the Aeneid that show iconographic
parallels with some of the illustrations in Vatican City, Biblioteca Apostolica
Vaticana, MS Vat. lat. 3225. Heslin argues that the poetry of Vergil, Horace,
and Propertius was influenced by paintings of the Trojan War. Venturini
compares a fourth-century mosaic depicting Aeneas and Turnus from the
House of Medusa at Alter do Chão, Portugal, with Aen. 12.919-52, the
passage it illustrates.
